comments.text: Update on a potential conjecture and verification for small numbers: For $1 \le N \le 9$ (verified fully) and $10 \le N \le 14$ (assuming there is a singleton set $\{c\}$ in the extremal family), the extrema for $t$ are $\binom{N}{2}+\lceil N/4 \rceil.$ These values correspond with the ones by the construction (end of paper) by [Szabo99]. For $N=13$, there are $5$ extremal families all containing $7$. (Szabo also mentioned his construction can be tweaked in multiple ways)
It sounds like you have generated some suitable sequences to submit to the OEIS (if they are not there already) and add to the new table linking the Erdos problems to the OEIS!

Reducing the number of comments, in conclusion: The conjectures of Erdos and Graham, as well as Simonovits and Sós are both false. The state of the art (based on its references in Google Scholar) is the work [Szabo99]. In particular, here it is proven (theorem 2.1) that $t \le (1+o(1)\frac {N^2}{2}$. Here, two questions are presented at the end: *Can one prove that every element of an extremal system contains a fixed integer $c$? *It is true that $t \le \frac {N^2}{2}+O(N)?$ (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I'm sorry to burst your bubble after all the hard work you've put into this, but I took a closer look at [SiSo81]. At the bottom of page 364, they point out that you can take all the sets of three or fewer elements where one of the elements is fixed. This yields $\binom{n}{2} + 1$ sets. They conjecture that this is the largest possible, and give some minor variants just before the end of the paper.

I now have also done some computations for small N. For fixed $N$, given the maximum value of $t$, there may be multiple families $\mathcal F= \{A_1, \ldots, A_t\}$ attaining equality. For $N=4$, there are $4$ ($14$ if not considered up to isomorphism) families with $t=7$. Two families with one element in common *[{3}, {1, 3}, {2, 3}, {3, 4}, {1, 2, 3}, {2, 3, 4}, {1, 2, 3, 4}] *[{1}, {1, 2}, {1, 3}, {1, 4}, {1, 2, 3}, {1, 2, 4}, {1, 3, 4}] Two families have no element in common: *[{1, 2}, {1, 3}, {1, 4}, {1, 2, 3}, {1, 2, 4}, {1, 3, 4}, {2, 3, 4}] *[{1, 2}, {1, 3}, {2, 3}, {1, 2, 3}, {1, 2, 4}, {1, 3, 4}, {2, 3, 4}] For $N=5$, only the conjectured family by Erdos and Graham with $k=3$. For $N=6$, there is only one extremal family up to isomorphism [{3}, {1, 3}, {2, 3}, {3, 4}, {3, 5}, {3, 6}, {1, 2, 3}, {1, 3, 5}, {1, 3, 6}, {2, 3, 4}, {2, 3, 6}, {3, 4, 5}, {3, 4, 6},{3, 5, 6}, {1, 2, 3, 4}, {2, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}] (so already not of the conjectured form) For $3 \le N \le 9$, the extrema are resp. $$\textbf{4},\textbf{7},\textbf{12},17,23,30,\textbf{39}$$ (bolt values are examples where the maximum is attained by, possibly among others, a family of arithmetic progressions containing some fixed element)

To clarify, Erdős and Graham made two separate conjectures: they conjectured that $t$ is maximized when we take the $A_i$ to be the arithmetic progressions containing some fixed element, and they conjectured that the amount of sets in this construction is maximized when the fixed element is $\left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor$. (Though perhaps they meant to say $\left\lceil \frac{N}{2} \right\rceil$?) If the fixed element is $k$, then the amount of progressions is \begin{equation*} \left(\sum_{d=1}^{N-1}\left\lceil\frac{k}{d}\right\rceil\left\lceil\frac{N-k+1}{d}\right\rceil\right) - N + 2. \end{equation*} In the above expression, the variable $d$ represents the difference of the progression, and the subtraction is to account for the fact that the singleton set is counted multiple times.

Since $\frac{N+1}{2}$ is the central element, $\lceil N/2 \rceil =\lfloor (N+1)/2 \rfloor$ would indeed be more natural, maximizing the main term where $d=1$. Nevertheless, the second conjecture is also false. The rounding has a big impact ($k-1$ having many prime factors should be advantageous, since rounding up $k/d$ implies the other factor is taken once more) E.g., when $N=29$, the optimum is attained in $k=13$ (and symmetrically in $k=17$).
